title: Chapter 17: The Palazzo
content: The vaporetto engine growls beneath my feet, a low diesel heartbeat, and the Grand Canal spreads before us like a vein of hammered pewter. Late November. Venice is half-drowned in fog — the kind of fog that doesn't drift so much as crouch, pressing its weight against the rooftops, blurring the palazzos into shapes that could be architecture or could be something risen from the lagoon floor, ancient and patient and waiting.

Rowan sits beside me on the wooden bench, his cashmere coat draped open, collar turned up against the wind that comes off the water, sharp and salt-lipped. He hasn't looked at me since we boarded. He's looking at the palazzos — at the Ca' d'Oro dissolving into mist, at the Rialto glimpsed like a half-formed thought through the grey — and his profile is a study in composure. The jaw set. The hands still on his knees.

Three days. Three days of emails about fabric orders and shipping timelines and whether the Murano beadwork on the sample bodice justified the cost. Three days of his voice in my ear saying *Ms. Orsino* and *regarding the Lagos delivery* and not a single word — not one — about the kiss. Not about the way he pressed his mouth to mine in the dark of the atelier. Not about the way I pulled back like he'd burned me.

Because he had. That's the thing no one tells you about touching the person whose soul has loved you across centuries: it doesn't feel like coming home. It feels like touching a live wire. Every cell in my body screamed *yes* and *him* and *finally*, and then the other scream — the self-preservation one, the one that knows what happens when I love Rowan Ashford — started up and I stepped back and said nothing.

He broke the silence. Yesterday morning, an email: *Fabric sourcing in Venice. Lagorce commitment. Two days. Carve it out of next week if you can.* No greeting. No warmth. Just work. And I typed *Yes* before I could think, because Venice, because my feet remember Venice, because sometimes the body makes decisions the mind is too cowardice to make.

Now I'm here and the fog smells like sea-salt and something older — something underneath the salt, the mineral smell of stone that's been wet for eight hundred years. The wind hits my face and I taste brine, and something in my chest cracks open like a fault line.
I know this city.
I lived here.
*Don't,* I tell myself. *Don't do this here.*

Too late. The palazzos are rising from the water, each one a face I recognize — not the specifics, the details have changed, but the bones of them, the way they stand with their marble feet in the canal like aging aristocrats refusing to leave a party that ended centuries ago. I lived in one of these. I loved in one of these. I watched the light of a November afternoon — another November, two hundred and eighty-three Novembers ago — go out of someone's eyes while the canal lapped at the watergate and I couldn't reach the doctor in time and his hand went slack in mine and—
"Your stop," Rowan says.

I blink. The vaporetto is slowing. San Stae. I stand, and the boat rocks, and my hand catches the rail, and the metal is cold and wet and real and I am here, now, Orlando, not Orsino, not whoever I was when this city broke me the first time.
Rowan follows me off the boat. His shoulder brushes mine in the press of disembarking passengers and I feel the contact like a brand through my jacket. He doesn't acknowledge it. I don't either.

The textile warehouse sits behind the church of San Stae, through a calle so narrow we walk single-file, and the fog presses in from both sides like hands cupping a candle flame. The warehouse belongs to the Manno family — four generations of Venetian fabric merchants, and when they unfold the bolts for us, I understand why.

The first is Venetian velvet. I touch it and my fingers sink, the pile catching the light like water catching the sun — shallow ripples of gold across a ground of deep burgundy, the cut pile so fine it breathes under my hand. I've felt this before. Not this specific bolt, but this technique, this centuries-old way of weaving two layers and cutting them apart so the pile stands like a forest of whispers. I used to commission this cloth. Marco used to watch me drape it across my arm and say I looked like a painting by Bellini, and I would laugh and tell him Bellini was overrated and Veronese understood color better, and he would pretend to be scandalized—
"Orsino."
Rowan's voice. I look up. His jaw is tight. He saw something in my face — the slip, the way I went somewhere else — and it bothers him, but he won't ask. He's not asking anything anymore.
"Sorry," I say. "It's beautiful."
"The lace," he says, and nods toward the next table.

Burano lace. Needle lace, worked by women on an island to the north, each motif built stitch by painstaking stitch — ponto in aria, ponto tagliato, ponto refe — the thread so fine it might be spider silk, might be spun moonlight. I lift the edge of a panel and the lace hangs from my fingers like a whisper made visible. My throat tightens.

Rowan watches my hands. His eyes track the movement of my fingers across the lace — the way I turn it, test the tension, hold it to the light — and he says nothing, but I can feel the distance in him, deliberate as architecture. He's building walls. Professional ones. Each email about fabric orders was a brick. Each *Ms. Orsino* was mortar.
He thinks the kiss was rejection.

The realization hits me sideways. Of course he does. I kissed him back and then I pulled away and I said nothing and nothing and nothing. He thinks I don't want him. He's retreating with dignity, pulling into himself like a creature that's learned the hard way that reaching out gets you struck.

And here's the acid joke of it: his distance is the only thing keeping my body from shifting. When he was close — when his mouth was on mine — I felt the tremor start, the deep-structural hum that means my form is trying to reorganize itself around the fact of him. Only Rowan does this. Only ever Rowan. The soul knows what the mind refuses, and the body obeys the soul, and if he touches me again I will shift, I will become, I will crack open like a frescoed wall revealing something underneath, and I cannot — I *cannot* — let that happen here.

So I let him retreat. I let the walls go up. I touch the fabrics like a woman doing her job. Silk the color of candlelight — I run my palm across it and feel the warp and weft like a language I used to speak. Rowan signs the orders. We shake hands with the Manno family. We walk out into the fog.

The calli of Venice are a labyrinth designed by a saint with a grudge. Narrow streets that bend and double back, dead-ending into canals, opening suddenly into tiny campi with wells in the center and cats watching from windowsills. The fog transforms them — walls become suggestions, archways become questions, and every footstep echoes off stone that's been walked on since the Republic was young.

I lead. I don't mean to. Rowan says, "Should we find a traghetto back to the hotel?" and I say, "This way," and my feet are already moving, tracing a path I couldn't draw on a map if you held a gun to my head but that my body knows the way my lungs know how to breathe. Left at the bridge with the worn step. Right past the church with the brass door — no, bronze, they replaced it after the flood of '66, but the arch is the same. Straight through the sottoportego, the covered passageway, where the stone arch drops low and the fog funnels through like breath through a throat.

Rowan follows. He doesn't question it. He follows the way you follow someone who seems to know where they're going, which is the most dangerous kind of trust, because I don't know where I'm going. My feet do. My feet are three hundred years old and they are taking me somewhere my mind has spent centuries trying to forget.
I turn a corner.
I stop dead.
The palazzo.

Three stories of Istrian stone and crumbling marble, its façade the color of old bone, windows like dark eyes beneath stone brows, the watergate at canal level standing open to the green-grey water. Scaffolding clings to the left side — metal bones, green netting, the architecture of renovation. A plaque beside the entrance, bronze letters against weathered stone:
**RESTORATION PROJECT FUNDED BY THE ASHFORD FOUNDATION**
Rowan's Foundation.
Rowan's money.
Preserving the building where I once lived. Where Marco died.

The fog sits on the palazzo's shoulders like a shawl. The marble steps leading up from the canal are slick with damp. The archway over the watergate — the archway where Marco used to tie up the gondola and call up to my window, *Orsino, come down, the light is extraordinary and you're wasting it on that painting* — that archway is the same. Exact. The same.
I can't breathe. The sound that comes out of me isn't a word. It's the sound of a body remembering what the mind tried to bury.

I'm standing in the doorway. I don't remember walking. The courtyard is smaller than I remember — everything is smaller, the well in the center, the stairs curving up, the archway to the canal — but it's the same. The same worn groove in the marble step, centuries of feet carving a dip. The same iron ring on the well where we tied the boat. The same light, grey and wet and Venetian, falling through the open archway and pooling on the stone floor like something poured.
The centuries collapse.

I am twenty-three. I am four hundred and twenty-three. I am standing in the courtyard of the palazzo that was my home, the place where I woke up next to someone who looked at me like I was the whole of art and music and mathematics compressed into a single person, and he was wrong about that — I was none of those things, I was just someone who loved him so completely that it rearranged the furniture of my soul — and he died here. In a room upstairs. In a bed with green silk curtains. He died with his hand in mine and his eyes on my face and he said *don't be sad* and I have been sad ever since. Every single day. For two hundred and eighty-three years.
I am home and home is a tomb.
"Orlando."
Rowan's voice. Close. His hand is near my elbow, not touching, hovering — the specific distance of someone who wants to reach for you but has learned he shouldn't.
"What is this place to you?"

I open my mouth. The truth is right there, right there, perched on my tongue like a bird that's been caged for centuries and just felt the door open: *I lived here. I loved someone who looked like you in this building. He had your eyes. He had your way of standing when you're confused — weight on the back foot, arms crossed, head tilted. He died in my arms and I couldn't save him and I have been walking through every life since then with a room inside me that I lock and don't enter because entering it means feeling that again and I cannot survive feeling that again—*
What comes out is: "I've been here before."
Four words. The most insufficient four words in the history of language.
Rowan looks at the palazzo. The scaffolding. The plaque. The crumbling marble and the wet stone and the centuries pressing in from every surface. Then he looks at me. Then back at the palazzo.

Something is computing behind his eyes. Not understanding — not yet — but the shape of it, the edges, the way a mathematician sees an equation and doesn't know the solution but knows the solution *exists*, knows it's real and close and waiting to be found.
"You said that like it meant more than tourism," he says.
My pulse is in my throat. "It does."

He doesn't push. I can see him want to — the question is right there, pressing against his teeth — but he holds it. He holds it the way he held my hand in the dark of the atelier, with a gentleness that costs him something.
Then he unbuttons his coat.

He takes it off. He drapes it over my shoulders, the cashmere settling against my back, and I realize I'm shivering — I've been shivering, maybe since I turned the corner, maybe since I got on the vaporetto, maybe since the day Marco's hand went slack in mine. I didn't notice. My body was too busy remembering to tell me it was cold.

The coat smells like him. Cedar and vetiver and the specific warmth of Rowan's skin, a scent I've catalogued without meaning to, the way you catalogue the smell of rain if you live in the desert. It settles around me like a held breath.

He's standing in his shirtsleeves in a Venetian courtyard in November, and he's not shivering, or he's pretending not to, and the gesture is so unthinking, so automatic — *she's cold, give her the coat* — that it bypasses every wall he's built and reveals the architecture underneath: he loves me. He's pulling away to protect himself, but the instinct to take care of me is older than his self-preservation, older than his dignity, older than every brick in every wall he's raised.
I pull the coat tighter. The cashmere is warm from his body. I breathe in. Cedar. Vetiver. Home, if I'm honest, if I'm reckless, if I let the bird out of the cage.

Behind us, inside the palazzo, past the courtyard and the scaffolding that crawls up the walls like metal ivy, a section of masonry has been opened for inspection — exposed brick, the rough red cores of walls that have held since the seventeenth century. And wedged into a gap between the bricks, sealed with wax the color of dried blood, is a letter.
The handwriting on the outside is mine. The date reads 1743.

I don't see it. Not yet. I'm standing in the house where Marco died, wearing the coat of the man his soul became, and the fog is closing over the canal like a hand over a mouth, and the letter is in the wall, and it has been waiting two hundred and eighty-three years, and it will change everything.
But not today.
